summary: Gruber shares a practical workaround for watching web video on Mac OS X without Flash Player installed: changing Safari's user agent string to impersonate iPad Mobile Safari, which causes websites to serve HTML5 video instead of Flash. He walks through the setup process, including creating a keyboard shortcut, and notes it works for Flickr, Vimeo, TED, MSNBC, and other sites that support iPad video. He then makes a strictly pragmatic case for uninstalling Flash, citing two undeniable facts: lower CPU usage and better video playback performance. He deliberately sidesteps the Apple-vs-Adobe political debate, arguing the practical benefits speak for themselves.
key_points: Changing Safari's user agent to iPad Mobile Safari forces websites to serve HTML5 video instead of Flash, proving desktop Safari is already capable of playing this content
Web developers should serve HTML5 video by default and fall back to Flash, not the other way around
Without Flash installed, Macs run cooler, faster, and with longer battery life — and HTML5 video playback is smoother than Flash video
Gruber deliberately frames his argument as practical rather than political, refusing to assign blame to Apple or Adobe
The argument that HTML5 animation will be just as bad as Flash is a variant of the fallacy that you shouldn't solve today's problem because a future problem might emerge
money_quotes: If it works on the iPad without Flash, it should work on a Mac without Flash.
This is not about politics, or any sort of idealism about how things should be.
Without Flash installed, my Mac uses less ambient CPU power. The machine runs cooler, faster, and the battery lasts longer.
My MacBook Pro's fan almost always kicks in when I play 720p video via Flash. It almost never kicks in when I play 720p video without Flash.

The argument that there's no use getting rid of Flash today because HTML5 animation might be just as big a problem in the future reminds me of the sort of people who argue that there's no use switching from Windows to Mac OS X to avoid malware because if everyone did it, the malware makers might switch to targeting Mac OS X.
Both Safari and my entire computer as a whole run better today than they did before I uninstalled Flash. Uninstall Flash on your Mac and see for yourself.
Web developers should start serving video via the HTML5 video tag by default, and fall back to Flash if the video tag isn't supported.
key_insight: The fact that a simple user-agent swap makes Flash-dependent video work perfectly in desktop Safari proves the web is already ready to move beyond Flash — the practical benefits of doing so are immediate and undeniable.
